reasoning: The timeline between the knowledge cutoff (April 25, 2025) and resolution date (July 1, 2025) is slightly over two months. While the Trump administration has escalated threats and even directed the IRS to make plans to revoke Harvard's 501(c)(3) status, the legal and administrative hurdles make actual revocation before July 1 nearly impossible. Under standard IRS procedures, revoking an organization's tax-exempt status requires a formal audit/examination, the issuance of a proposed revocation letter, a 30-day period for the organization to protest, and an appeals process, before a final determination letter can be issued. This process typically takes many months or years. If the administration attempts to bypass these rules (e.g., via an Executive Order or immediate decree), Harvard's formidable legal team would instantly seek a Temporary Restraining Order (TRO) and preliminary injunction. Because federal law (26 U.S.C. § 7217) prohibits the President from directly ordering IRS audits or investigations of specific taxpayers, and given the requirements of the Administrative Procedure Act, courts are highly likely to grant an injunction blocking any expedited or irregular revocation from taking effect while litigation proceeds. Thus, the legal mechanics guarantee that this process will stretch far beyond July 2025.
